Global Workspace Theory.description: Consciousness arises when information is broadcast globally across the brain through a central workspace accessible to multiple cognitive processes. Contents of consciousness are those that win competition for access to this global workspace, enabling wide broadcasting and coordination.
references: Baars, B.J. (1988). A Cognitive Theory of Consciousness. Cambridge University Press.
Dehaene, S. & Changeux, J.P. (2011). Experimental and theoretical approaches to conscious processing. Neuron, 70(2), 200-227. DOI: 10.1016/j.neuron.2011.03.018

Integrated Information Theory.description: Consciousness is identical to integrated information (Phi). A system is conscious to the degree it integrates information irreducibly across its parts. Different conscious states correspond to distinct conceptual structures; the substrate is any system with high Phi regardless of implementation.
references: Tononi, G. (2004). An information integration theory of consciousness. BMC Neuroscience, 5, 42. DOI: 10.1186/1471-2202-5-42

Tononi, G., Boly, M., Massimini, M., & Koch, C. (2016). Integrated information theory: from consciousness to its physical substrate. Nature Reviews Neuroscience, 17(7), 450-461. DOI: 10.1038/nrn.2016.44

Higher-Order Thought Theory.description: A mental state is conscious when it is accompanied by a suitable higher-order representation (thought or perception) directed at that state. Consciousness is not intrinsic to first-order states but requires meta-representation; without a higher-order state, the first-order state is merely unconscious.
references: Rosenthal, D.M. (2005). Consciousness and Mind. Oxford University Press.
Brown, R., Lau, H., & LeDoux, J.E. (2019). Understanding the Higher-Order Approach to Consciousness. Trends in Cognitive Sciences, 23(9), 754-768. DOI: 10.1016/j.tics.2019.06.003

Quantum Orchestrated Reduction.description: Consciousness arises from quantum computations in neuronal microtubules undergoing orchestrated objective reduction (Orch OR). Quantum superposition and wavefunction collapse in tubulin proteins generate moments of proto-conscious experience that cannot be replicated by classical computation.
references: Penrose, R. (1989). The Emperor's New Mind. Oxford University Press.
Hameroff, S. & Penrose, R. (2014). Consciousness in the universe: a review of the 'Orch OR' theory. Physics of Life Reviews, 11(1), 39-78. DOI: 10.1016/j.plrev.2013.08.002

Predictive Processing.description: The brain is a hierarchical prediction machine that continually generates and updates a generative model of the world. Conscious experience corresponds to the brain's best current hypothesis minimizing prediction error (free energy), integrating top-down predictions with bottom-up sensory signals through precision-weighted belief updating.
references: Clark, A. (2016). Surfing Uncertainty: Prediction, Action, and the Embodied Mind. Oxford University Press.
Friston, K. (2010). The free-energy principle: a unified brain theory? Nature Reviews Neuroscience, 11(2), 127-138. DOI: 10.1038/nrn2787
Hohwy, J. (2013). The Predictive Mind. Oxford University Press.

Attention Schema Theory.description: The brain builds a simplified internal model (attention schema) of its own attentional processes. Consciousness is this schema - a model that ascribes awareness to the self. It evolved to allow better control of attention and social attribution of mental states to others.
references: Graziano, M.S.A. (2013). Consciousness and the Social Brain. Oxford University Press.
Graziano, M.S.A. & Kastner, S. (2011). Human consciousness and its relationship to social neuroscience. Cognitive Neuroscience, 2(2), 98-113. DOI: 10.1080/17588928.2011.565121

Quantum Information Panpsychism.description: Consciousness is a fundamental property of systems in quantum pure states. Because quantum information cannot be cloned, each conscious system has private, first-person experience irreducible to third-person description. Free will emerges from irreducibly quantum indeterminacy not reducible to classical information processing.
references: Chalmers, D.J. (1996). The Conscious Mind: In Search of a Fundamental Theory. Oxford University Press.
Stapp, H.P. (2007). Mind, Matter and Quantum Mechanics. Springer.

Recurrent Processing Theory.description: Consciousness depends on recurrent (re-entrant) processing between higher and lower visual areas. Feedforward sweeps suffice for rapid, unconscious categorization; phenomenal experience requires sustained recurrent loops that feed information back to early sensory areas.
references: Lamme, V.A.F. (2006). Towards a true neural stance on consciousness. Trends in Cognitive Sciences, 10(11), 494-501. DOI: 10.1016/j.tics.2006.09.001
Lamme, V.A.F. & Roelfsema, P.R. (2000). The distinct modes of vision offered by feedforward and recurrent processing. Trends in Neurosciences, 23(11), 571-579.

Biological Naturalism.description: Consciousness is a real biological phenomenon caused by lower-level neuronal processes in the brain and realized in the brain's physical structure. It is irreducible to functional or computational descriptions; its subjective, first-person ontology cannot be captured by any third-person physicalist account based solely on syntax or computation.
references: Searle, J.R. (1992). The Rediscovery of the Mind. MIT Press.
Searle, J.R. (2000). Consciousness. Annual Review of Neuroscience, 23, 557-578. DOI: 10.1146/annurev.neuro.23.1.557

Illusionism.description: Phenomenal consciousness - the felt quality of experience - is an introspective illusion. The brain generates systematically misleading representations of its own mental states that make them appear to have irreducible qualitative properties (qualia). Explaining the illusion dissolves the hard problem.
references: Frankish, K. (2016). Illusionism as a theory of consciousness. Journal of Consciousness Studies, 23(11-12), 11-39.
Dennett, D.C. (1991). Consciousness Explained. Little, Brown and Company.

Active Inference and Free Energy.description: Conscious experience is the product of hierarchical Bayesian inference that minimizes variational free energy (a bound on surprisal). The brain encodes probabilistic beliefs about causes of sensory data; action and perception are unified as dual strategies for minimizing surprise under a generative model of the world and the agent's place in it.
references: Friston, K. (2019). A free energy principle for a particular physics. arXiv:1906.10184.
Friston, K., Wiese, W., & Hobson, J.A. (2020). Sentience and the free-energy principle. DOI: 10.31234/osf.io/p3nvz

Phenomenal Concepts Strategy.description: The explanatory gap between physical and phenomenal descriptions arises from the special way humans form phenomenal concepts - direct, non-inferential recognitional concepts applied to one's own experiences. These concepts are not a priori deducible from physical concepts, but this epistemic gap does not entail an ontological gap; physicalism remains tenable.
references: Loar, B. (1990). Phenomenal states. Philosophical Perspectives, 4, 81-108.
Papineau, D. (2002). Thinking about Consciousness. Oxford University Press.
